does: This paper formulates fine-tuning diffusion models with learned reward functions as a continuous-time stochastic control problem, treating score-matching functions as actionable controls. The authors develop a unified framework that enables reinforcement learning algorithms to optimize diffusion model generation quality by working within the continuous-time perspective of the diffusion process. They establish corresponding RL theory for policy optimization and regularization specifically designed for stochastic differential equation environments, providing rigorous theoretical foundations for applying RL to diffusion model alignment that go beyond previous heuristic approaches to RLHF in generative models.
connects: This work builds on the emerging intersection of reinforcement learning from human feedback and diffusion models, extending prior RLHF techniques originally developed for language models to the image generation domain. It responds to recent explorations of alignment in diffusion generative models by providing the theoretical rigor previously lacking in those applications. The paper connects to classical continuous-time stochastic control theory and diffusion process mathematics, bridging these established fields with modern generative model training. It also engages with score-based diffusion model theory, leveraging score functions as the natural control interface within the diffusion framework.
enables: By formalizing the connection between diffusion model fine-tuning and continuous-time RL, this framework opens pathways for applying sophisticated control-theoretic techniques to generative model alignment. It enables more principled development of RL algorithms specifically optimized for diffusion processes, potentially leading to more stable and sample-efficient training methods. The theoretical foundations could support future work on convergence guarantees, regularization strategies, and policy optimization techniques tailored to the diffusion setting. Additionally, this continuous-time perspective may extend to other generative models and could facilitate deeper investigation of how reward signals propagate through stochastic generation processes, advancing the field of generative model alignment more broadly.
